sections.heading: In this Act, unless the context otherwise requires —
sections.title: Definitions for legal and financial terms
sections.summary: This section clarifies the meanings of specific terms used in the previous section, including 'advocate and solicitor', 'practising certificate', 'regulated foreign lawyer', 'Official Assignee', 'Official Receiver', 'sub-fund', and 'VCC' as defined in relevant Acts such as the Legal Profession Act 1966 and Variable Capital Companies Act 2018.

requirements.text: "advocate and solicitor", "practising certificate" and "regulated foreign lawyer" have the meanings given by section 2(1) of the Legal Profession Act 1966
"Official Assignee" and "Official Receiver" have the meanings given by section 2(1) of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018
"sub-fund" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Variable Capital Companies Act 2018
"VCC" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Variable Capital Companies Act 2018

sections.source_quote: 2. For the purpose of paragraph 1 — "advocate and solicitor", "practising certificate" and "regulated foreign lawyer" have the meanings given by section 2(1) of the Legal Profession Act 1966; "Official Assignee" and "Official Receiver" have the meanings given by section 2(1) of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018; "sub-fund" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Variable Capital Companies Act 2018; "VCC" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Variable Capital Companies Act 2018.

sections.title: Prohibition orders and financial institution compliance
sections.summary: A prohibition order restricts a person from carrying out regulated activities or functions. Financial institutions must not employ or enter into arrangements with prohibited persons, or use their services, for regulated activities. Violations incur fines up to $150,000 for individuals and $100,000 for institutions. Financial institutions may claim defense if they took reasonable steps to ensure compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Prohibition orders restrict financial institutions from engaging with prohibited persons, requiring DTIA to assess risks of indirect compliance with prohibited entities across borders.

requirements.text: A person against whom a prohibition order is made must comply with the order
Financial institutions must not employ or enter into arrangements with prohibited persons for regulated activities
Financial institutions must not use prohibited persons' services for regulated activities
Financial institutions must take reasonable steps to ensure compliance with prohibition orders
Prohibited persons must inform representatives of prohibition orders
Representatives must cease activities prohibited by the order

sections.source_quote: A person against whom a prohibition order is made must comply with the prohibition order. Where a prohibition order is made against a person (A), a financial institution must not employ or enter into any arrangement with A, or use A's service, whether directly or indirectly — to carry on any activity or business, or provide any service, the carrying on or provision of which is regulated or authorised by the Authority
sections.relevance_reason: financial compliance with prohibition orders

sections.heading: A prohibition order, or any variation or revocation of a prohibition order, takes
sections.title: Effective date of prohibition orders
sections.summary: Prohibition orders or their variations/revocations take effect on the date specified by the Authority in the order or notice, as per section 9(1). This ensures timely implementation of restrictions on financial activities.
sections.dtia_summary: The effective date of prohibition orders impacts DTIA timelines for assessing cross-border compliance with restricted entities.

requirements.text: Prohibition orders take effect on the date specified by the Authority in the order or notice

sections.source_quote: A prohibition order, or any variation or revocation of a prohibition order, takes effect on the date specified by the Authority in the order or the notice in section 9(1), as the case may be.
sections.relevance_reason: commencement date

sections.heading: The Authority —
sections.title: Public disclosure of prohibition orders
sections.summary: The Authority must publish prohibition orders and their variations/revocations to ensure public awareness. The Authority may also publish additional information for investor protection or public interest.
sections.dtia_summary: Public disclosure requirements affect DTIA transparency for cross-border data flows involving prohibited entities.

requirements.text: The Authority must publish prohibition orders and variations/revocations in a manner ensuring adequate publicity
The Authority may publish additional information for public interest or investor protection

sections.source_quote: The Authority — (a) must publish the making of a prohibition order under section 7, and the variation or revocation of a prohibition order under section 9, in such manner as the Authority thinks will secure adequate publicity for the fact that the prohibition order was made, varied or revoked, as the case may be
sections.relevance_reason: Public disclosure of prohibition orders not data-related

sections.heading: The Authority — (cont.)
sections.title: Definitions for AML/CFT and Financial Supervision
sections.summary: This section defines key terms including 'AML/CFT authority', 'foreign financial institution', 'protected information', and 'supervision' in the context of Singapore's financial regulatory framework. It establishes the meaning of terms critical for cross-border financial supervision and anti-money laundering activities, particularly relating to foreign financial institutions and their compliance with international requirements.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines critical terms for cross-border financial data transfers under AML/CFT frameworks, specifying what constitutes foreign financial institutions and supervision mechanisms that may impact data sharing between Singapore and foreign jurisdictions.

requirements.text: The Authority may provide assistance to an AML/CFT authority of a foreign country if the Authority is satisfied that all conditions are fulfilled
The AML/CFT authority must provide written undertakings on confidentiality and non-disclosure of information
The request must specify the purpose, identity of the financial institution, and relevance of information to supervision
The type and amount of information requested must be proportionate to the supervisory action
The matter must be of sufficient gravity
The AML/CFT authority must give undertaking to comply with future requests
The assistance must not be contrary to national or public interest

sections.source_quote: The Authority may, on the request of an AML/CFT authority of a foreign country, provide the assistance referred to in section 20 to the AML/CFT authority, if the Authority is satisfied that all of the following conditions are fulfilled
sections.relevance_reason: AML/CFT definitions critical for cross-border financial data transfers

sections.heading: The purposes of this Division are —
sections.title: Purpose of Assistance for AML/CFT Authorities
sections.summary: This section outlines the purposes of the Division, which include enabling the Authority to share information with foreign AML/CFT authorities for supervision of foreign financial institutions, supporting investigations into applicable offences, and facilitating inspections of financial institutions in Singapore by foreign AML/CFT authorities. It establishes the scope of information sharing for regulatory compliance purposes.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies the conditions under which Singapore's financial regulator can share data with foreign AML/CFT authorities for supervision, investigations, and inspections, directly impacting cross-border data transfer for financial compliance.

requirements.text: The Authority provides information to foreign AML/CFT authorities for supervision of foreign financial institutions
The Authority provides information to domestic authorities for investigations into applicable offences
The Authority provides information to domestic authorities for enforcement actions
The Authority provides information to domestic authorities for supervisory actions

sections.source_quote: The purposes of this Division are — to enable the Authority to provide information to an AML/CFT authority of a foreign country in connection with the AML/CFT authority's supervision of foreign financial institutions carrying on any financial activities in that country for compliance with the AML/CFT requirements of that country applicable to those institutions
sections.relevance_reason: Cross-border AML data sharing for compliance

sections.heading: The Authority may, on the request of a domestic authority, provide the assistance
sections.title: Conditions for Providing Domestic Authority Assistance
sections.summary: This section details the conditions that must be met for the Authority to provide assistance to domestic authorities, including the requirement for the request to be received after commencement, the purpose of the assistance, and the proportionality of information requests. It specifies that the matter must be of sufficient gravity for assistance to be granted.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets conditions for data sharing with domestic authorities for investigations and enforcement actions, directly impacting cross-border data transfer impact assessments for financial institutions.

requirements.text: The request must be received by the Authority on or after the commencement date
The assistance must be intended for investigations, enforcement actions, or supervisory actions
The type and amount of information requested must be proportionate to the action
The matter must be of sufficient gravity

sections.source_quote: The Authority may, on the request of a domestic authority, provide the assistance referred to in section 22 to the domestic authority, if the Authority is satisfied that all of the following conditions, and all such other conditions as the Authority may determine, are fulfilled
sections.relevance_reason: Regulator powers for financial data requests

sections.heading: Despite the provisions of any prescribed written law or any requirement imposed
sections.title: AML/CFT authority inspection powers for financial institutions
sections.summary: The Authority may authorize AML/CFT authorities to inspect financial institutions' books and information in Singapore for consolidated supervision purposes. Inspections require written approval from the Authority, confidentiality undertakings, and adherence to specific conditions including information access limitations and use restrictions. Financial institutions must cooperate with inspections but may request reasonable business continuity. Non-compliance carries fines up to $100,000.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for cross-border AML/CFT inspections requiring confidentiality safeguards and data access restrictions that impact data transfer assessments for financial institutions.

requirements.text: AML/CFT authority may inspect financial institution books with Authority's written approval
AML/CFT authority must provide written undertaking to protect confidentiality
AML/CFT authority must comply with conditions imposed by Authority
Financial institution must provide access to books and information for inspection
Financial institution may request reasonable business continuity during inspections
Non-compliance with inspection requirements carries fines up to $100,000

sections.source_quote: 26.—(1) An AML/CFT authority may, with the prior written approval of the Authority and under conditions of secrecy, conduct an inspection in Singapore of the books of a financial institution in accordance with this section, if all of the following conditions are satisfied: (a) the financial institution is one over which the AML/CFT authority exercises consolidated supervision authority, and the inspection is solely for the purpose of such consolidated supervision; (b) the AML/CFT authority — (i) is prohibited by the laws applicable to it from disclosing information obtained by it in the course of the inspection to any other person, except when compelled to do so by the laws or a court of the country or territory where it is established; or (ii) has given to the Authority such written undertaking to protect the confidentiality of the information obtained as the Authority may require; (c) the AML/CFT authority has given a written undertaking to the Authority to comply with such conditions as the Authority may impose under subsection (3); (d) the AML/CFT authority has provided or is willing to provide similar assistance to the Authority.
sections.relevance_reason: AML/CFT inspections directly impact financial data compliance

sections.heading: Despite the provisions of any prescribed written law or any requirement imposed (cont.)
sections.title: Continuation of AML/CFT inspection provisions
sections.summary: This section continues the AML/CFT inspection framework by detailing penalties for non-compliance with inspection requirements. Financial institutions that refuse to cooperate face fines up to $100,000 per day for continuing offenses. The section also establishes defenses for those who act with reasonable care and good faith to comply with inspection obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies penalties for non-compliance with AML/CFT inspections that affect data transfer assessments through financial institution penalties and compliance requirements.

requirements.text: Financial institution that refuses inspection cooperation faces fines up to $100,000
Continuing offenses incur additional fines of $10,000 per day
Acting with reasonable care and good faith constitutes defense against penalties

sections.source_quote: 27.—(4) A financial institution that, without reasonable excuse, refuses or neglects to comply with subsection (1) shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $100,000 and, in the case of a continuing offence, to a further fine not exceeding $10,000 for every day or part of a day during which the offence continues after conviction.
sections.relevance_reason: Penalties for non-compliance with AML/CFT inspections

sections.heading: In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires —
sections.title: Definitions for dispute resolution schemes
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the dispute resolution schemes framework including 'approved dispute resolution scheme', 'operator', and 'terms of reference'. These definitions establish the scope and application of dispute resolution mechanisms for financial institutions.

requirements.text: Approved dispute resolution scheme means scheme approved by MAS under section 31(1)
Operator means person administering dispute resolution scheme
Terms of reference define scope, application, operations and procedures of dispute resolution scheme

sections.source_quote: 30. In this Part, unless the context otherwise requires — "approved dispute resolution scheme" means a dispute resolution scheme approved by the Authority under section 31(1); "operator", in relation to a dispute resolution scheme approved under section 31(1), means a person who administers a dispute resolution scheme; "terms of reference", in relation to an approved dispute resolution scheme, means the terms which define the scope, application, operations and procedures of the approved dispute resolution scheme.

sections.heading: An operator, or a chief executive officer or director of an operator, who is
sections.title: Appeals to Minister for dispute resolution scheme decisions
sections.summary: Operators or chief executive officers of dispute resolution schemes may appeal decisions by the Monetary Authority of Singapore to the Minister within 30 days. The Minister's decision is final.

requirements.text: Operators or chief executive officers may appeal MAS decisions to Minister within 30 days

sections.source_quote: 34. An operator, or a chief executive officer or director of an operator, who is aggrieved by a decision of the Authority under section 33(1) may, within 30 days after receiving the direction, appeal in writing to the Minister, whose decision is final.

sections.title: Constitution amendments for dispute resolution scheme operators
sections.summary: The Monetary Authority of Singapore requires prior approval for constitutional amendments by dispute resolution scheme operators that are companies. This ensures regulatory oversight of organizational changes.

requirements.text: Company operators must obtain MAS approval before amending constitution

sections.source_quote: 35. It is a condition of an approval for any dispute resolution scheme approved under section 31(1) that the operator of the approved dispute resolution scheme that is a company must not amend its constitution without the prior approval of the Authority.

sections.heading: No liability shall lie against any mediator, adjudicator or employee of an operator
sections.title: No liability for dispute resolution staff acting with care and good faith
sections.summary: This section states that mediators, adjudicators, and employees of an approved dispute resolution scheme are not liable for actions taken with reasonable care and good faith during dispute resolution. The liability protection applies to those acting within the scope of their duties under the approved scheme. The section does not specify who the operator is or what constitutes reasonable care and good faith.

requirements.text: No liability shall lie against any mediator, adjudicator or employee of an operator of an approved dispute resolution scheme for actions taken with reasonable care and good faith in dispute resolution

sections.source_quote: No liability shall lie against any mediator, adjudicator or employee of an operator of an approved dispute resolution scheme, for doing or omitting to do any act, if the act is done or omitted to be done with reasonable care and in good faith in the course of or in connection with any mediation or adjudication of a dispute under the approved dispute resolution scheme.

sections.title: Financial institution officers' obligations during authority control
sections.summary: During the Authority's or statutory manager's control of a relevant financial institution's business, court-ordered persons must surrender property, records, and documents to the Authority or statutory manager within specified timeframes. Individuals must also provide information required for the Authority's or statutory manager's duties. Failure to comply without reasonable excuse or providing false/misleading information constitutes an offence punishable by fines up to $125,000 and imprisonment for up to 3 years.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires financial institutions to transfer records and information to regulators during control events, impacting data transfer and retention during regulatory interventions.

requirements.text: Court may direct persons to surrender property, records, or documents to Authority or statutory manager within specified period
Persons must provide information required for Authority or statutory manager's duties within specified time and manner
Failure to comply without reasonable excuse constitutes an offence punishable by fines up to $125,000 and imprisonment up to 3 years
Providing false or misleading information knowingly or recklessly constitutes an offence punishable by fines up to $125,000 and imprisonment up to 3 years

sections.source_quote: During the period when the Authority or statutory manager is in control of the relevant business of a relevant financial institution — (a) the Court may, on an application by the Authority or statutory manager, direct any person... to pay, deliver, convey, surrender or transfer to the Authority or statutory manager... (b) any person... must give to the Authority or statutory manager such information as the Authority or statutory manager may require... A person who — (a) without reasonable excuse, fails to comply with subsection (1)(b); or (b) in purported compliance with subsection (1)(b), knowingly or recklessly provides any information or document that is false or misleading in a material particular, shall be guilty of an offence...

sections.title: Remuneration for statutory managers and authorities
sections.summary: The Authority may determine remuneration and expenses for statutory managers or statutory advisers appointed to relevant financial institutions, whether appointments have been revoked. The Authority may also set remuneration for itself and persons appointed under section 179 when it has assumed control of a financial institution's business.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines financial institution remuneration obligations during regulatory interventions but has minimal direct impact on data transfer impact assessments.

requirements.text: Authority may fix remuneration and expenses for statutory manager or statutory adviser appointed to relevant financial institution
Authority may fix remuneration and expenses for itself and persons appointed under section 179 when it has assumed control of relevant financial institution's business

sections.source_quote: The Authority may at any time fix the remuneration and expenses to be paid by a relevant financial institution — (a) to a statutory manager or statutory adviser appointed in relation to the relevant financial institution, whether or not the appointment has been revoked; and (b) where the Authority has assumed control of the relevant business of the relevant financial institution, to the Authority and any person appointed by the Authority under section 179 in relation to the Authority's assumption of control of the relevant business

sections.title: Prohibition on appointing directors or executives with disqualifying convictions
sections.summary: Relevant financial institutions in Singapore must not appoint directors or executive officers without prior written consent from the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS). Appointments are prohibited if the person has been convicted of fraud or dishonesty, is an undischarged bankrupt, has an enforcement order against them, has entered into a compromise with creditors, has a prohibition order, or has been a director of a regulated financial institution that has been wound up or had its license revoked. The Authority may remove such individuals if they have wilfully contravened the Act or failed to ensure compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes restrictions on appointing directors/executives with disqualifying convictions or financial misconduct, directly impacting cross-border appointments of personnel for financial institutions operating in Singapore.

requirements.text: Relevant financial institution must not permit a person to act as its executive officer without prior written consent of the Authority
Relevant financial institution must not permit a person to act as its director without prior written consent of the Authority
Person must not have been convicted of fraud or dishonesty in Singapore or elsewhere
Person must not be an undischarged bankrupt in Singapore or elsewhere
Person must not have an enforcement order against them for a judgment debt returned unsatisfied
Person must not have entered into a compromise or scheme of arrangement with creditors that is still in operation
Person must not have a prohibition order
Person must not have been a director of a regulated financial institution that has been wound up or had its license revoked
Authority may direct removal of director or executive officer if they have wilfully contravened the Act
Authority may direct removal of director or executive officer if they have failed to secure compliance with the Act
Authority must give written notice before directing removal of director or executive officer
Authority must allow relevant financial institution and person to show cause why they should not be removed

sections.source_quote: a relevant financial institution must not, without the prior written consent of the Authority, permit a person to act as its executive officer; and a relevant financial institution which is established or incorporated in Singapore must not, without the prior written consent of the Authority, permit a person to act as its director

sections.title: Conditions for compromise arrangements in financial institutions
sections.summary: The Authority regulates compromise or arrangement procedures for financial institutions undergoing insolvency proceedings. For Type A institutions, the Authority has creditor rights but cannot vote in meetings. For Type B institutions, the Court cannot approve compromises involving transfers of assets without Minister consent. For Type C institutions, the Court cannot approve compromises without Authority consent.
sections.dtia_summary: This section governs the conditions under which financial institutions can enter into compromise arrangements during insolvency, affecting cross-border insolvency procedures for Singapore-based financial entities.

requirements.text: Authority has same powers and rights as a creditor in insolvency proceedings
Authority does not have voting rights in meetings of Type A financial institutions
Court cannot approve compromise arrangements involving asset transfers for Type B financial institutions without Minister consent
Court cannot approve compromise arrangements involving asset transfers for Type C financial institutions without Authority consent

sections.source_quote: In the case of a company that is a Type C financial institution, the Court must not approve under section 210 of the Companies Act 1967 or section 71 of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2: any compromise or arrangement that has been proposed for the purposes of or in connection with any scheme mentioned in section 212(1) of the Companies Act 1967 under which the whole or any part of the undertaking or the property of the company is to be transferred
sections.relevance_reason: Tangential governance for financial institutions

sections.heading: Regulations made under section 192 for this Part may prescribe —
sections.title: Penalties for regulatory non-compliance
sections.summary: Regulations under section 192 may prescribe offences for contraventions of the regulations, with fines up to $250,000 for individuals and $250,000 for financial institutions, plus continuing fines of $25,000 per day for ongoing violations. The regulations may also specify different persons and classes of persons for different financial institutions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes penalties for non-compliance with regulatory requirements, directly impacting financial institutions' compliance with data and operational safeguards.

requirements.text: Contravention of regulations may be punishable by a fine not exceeding $250,000 and continuing fines of $25,000 per day

sections.source_quote: Regulations made under section 192 for this Part may prescribe that any contravention of a provision of the regulations shall be an offence punishable (despite section 192(2)(d)) — (i) in the case of an individual, with a fine not exceeding $125,000 or with imprisonment for a term not exceeding 3 years or with both and, in the case of a continuing offence, with a further fine not exceeding $12,500 for every day or part of a day during which the offence continues after conviction; or (ii) in any other case, with a fine not exceeding $250,000 and, in the case of a continuing offence, with a further fine not exceeding $25,000 for every day or part of a day during which the offence continues after conviction

These definitions establish the scope and meaning of terms for regulatory purposes in Singapore's financial sector.

sections.heading: In determining whether to exercise its powers under Divisions 2, 4, 5 and 6 of this
sections.title: Criteria for exercising powers over financial institutions
sections.summary: This section outlines the factors the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) may consider when determining whether to exercise powers under Divisions 2, 4, 5, and 6 of Part O. These include the potential widespread adverse effects on Singapore's financial system or economy from a pertinent financial institution's failure, whether such action serves the public interest, and any other matters deemed relevant by MAS. The section applies to pertinent financial institutions and focuses on systemic risk assessment.

requirements.text: The Authority may have regard to whether a failure of the pertinent financial institution would have a widespread adverse effect on the financial system in Singapore or the economy of Singapore, or both
The Authority may have regard to whether it is in the public interest to exercise powers
The Authority may have regard to any other matter that the Authority considers relevant

sections.source_quote: 59. In determining whether to exercise its powers under Divisions 2, 4, 5 and 6 of this Part in relation to a pertinent financial institution, the Authority may have regard to one or more of the following matters: (a) whether a failure of the pertinent financial institution would have a widespread adverse effect on the financial system in Singapore or the economy of Singapore, or both, whether or not that widespread adverse effect occurs directly or indirectly as a result of the impact of the failure on the financial system in Singapore, on the financial markets in Singapore or on other financial institutions in Singapore; (b) whether it is in the public interest to do so; (c) any other matter that the Authority considers relevant.
sections.relevance_reason: MAS systemic risk assessment for financial institutions

sections.heading: In this Division, unless the context otherwise requires —
sections.title: Definitions for business transfers in financial services
sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in business transfers between financial institutions. It specifies that 'business' includes affairs, property, rights, obligations and liabilities. 'Transferor' refers to a financial institution transferring business, while 'transferee' is the recipient. The section also defines 'specified business', 'guaranteed policy moneys', and 'determination' as used in the compulsory transfer process. These definitions establish the foundational terminology for the transfer procedures outlined in subsequent sections.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines critical terms for cross-border business transfers between financial institutions, establishing the scope of what constitutes a transferable business and the key parties involved in the transfer process.

requirements.text: Business includes affairs, property, right, obligation and liability
Certificate means a certificate of transfer issued by the Minister under section 67(1)
Debenture has the meaning given by section 4(1) of the Companies Act 1967
Determination means a determination made by the Authority under section 66(1)
Guaranteed policy moneys has the meaning given by section 2 of the Insurance Act 1966
Property includes property, right and power of every description
Specified business means any part of the business of a transferor specified or identified in a certificate
Transferee means any person to which the whole or any part of a transferor's business is transferred
Transferor means a pertinent financial institution the whole or any part of whose business is transferred

sections.source_quote: In this Division, unless the context otherwise requires — "business" includes affairs, property, right, obligation and liability; "certificate" means a certificate of transfer issued by the Minister under section 67(1); "debenture" has the meaning given by section 4(1) of the Companies Act 1967; "determination" means a determination made by the Authority under section 66(1); "guaranteed policy moneys" has the meaning given by section 2 of the Insurance Act 1966; "property" includes property, right and power of every description; "specified business" means any part of the business of a transferor which is specified or identified in a certificate; "transferee" means any person to which the whole or any part of a transferor's business is, is to be, or is proposed to be transferred under this Division; "transferor" means a pertinent financial institution the whole or any part of the business of which is, is to be, or is proposed to be transferred under this Division.

sections.summary: This section defines key terms used in the context of business transfers between financial institutions, including 'transferor', 'transferee', 'certificate of transfer', 'onward transfer', and 'reverse transfer'. These definitions establish the framework for understanding how business transfers occur under the Act, particularly when financial institutions transfer parts of their operations to other entities.
sections.dtia_summary: These definitions clarify the terminology for business transfers, which is critical for DTIAs to understand the scope of transfers and the mechanisms for reverse transfers between financial institutions.

requirements.text: 2nd transferee means the person to which the whole or part of a transferor's business that was transferred to a transferee by a certificate of transfer is or is to be transferred from the transferee in accordance with an onward transfer certificate under section 73
business includes affairs, property, right, obligation and liability

sections.title: Minister's approval process for share transfers
sections.summary: The Minister must approve determinations made by the Authority regarding share transfers between financial institutions. The Minister publishes a notice in the Gazette and provides written notice to the transferor before approving the determination. The Minister considers written representations from the transferor and may approve the determination without modification, subject to modifications agreed by the transferee, or refuse approval. The Minister must ensure the transfer does not compromise financial system stability.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates formal approval by the Minister for share transfers involving financial institutions, requiring public notice and written representations. It imposes temporary restrictions on the transferor's actions during the approval process to ensure financial stability.

requirements.text: The Minister must publish a notice in the Gazette and newspapers before approving the determination
The Minister must provide written notice to the transferor specifying the date for written representations
The Minister must consider written representations from the transferor when deciding on approval
The Minister may approve the determination without modification
The Minister may approve the determination subject to modifications agreed by the transferee
The Minister may refuse to approve the determination

sections.source_quote: The Minister must publish in the Gazette and in such newspaper or newspapers as the Minister may determine a notice of the Minister's intention to approve the determination, specifying such particulars as the Minister considers appropriate and the date by which the transferor may make written representations to the Minister.

sections.title: Restrictions on financial institution shares during transfer
sections.summary: This section imposes temporary restrictions on the financial institution's assets and operations during the share transfer process. These restrictions include halting enforcement actions, sales, transfers, voting rights, and dividend payments related to the transferred shares. The restrictions apply to ensure financial stability and prevent adverse effects on the institution.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates temporary restrictions on the financial institution's operations during share transfers to protect financial stability, including prohibitions on enforcement actions, sales, and voting rights for the transferred shares.

requirements.text: No enforcement orders or legal processes may be commenced against the transferred shares
No steps may be taken to enforce security over the transferred shares
Any sale, transfer, or assignment of the transferred shares is void
No voting rights may be exercised for the transferred shares unless the Minister permits
No payments may be made by the financial institution for the transferred shares unless the Minister authorizes
No winding up proceedings may be initiated for the financial institution

sections.source_quote: No enforcement order or other legal process may be commenced or continued against the share; no steps may be taken to enforce any security over the share; any sale, transfer, assignment or other disposition of the share is void.

Property includes property, right and power of every description
Significant shareholder means any person prescribed by regulations as a significant shareholder

sections.source_quote: In this Division, unless the context otherwise requires, 'business' includes affairs, property, right, obligation and liability; 'certificate' means a certificate of restructuring of share capital issued by the Minister under section 79(1); 'determination' means a determination made by the Authority under section 78(1) or (2); 'property' includes property, right and power of every description; 'significant shareholder', in relation to a pertinent financial institution, means any person prescribed by regulations made under section 135 as a significant shareholder for that pertinent financial institution; 'significant shareholder provisions', in relation to any pertinent financial institution, means such provisions of written law as may be prescribed by regulations made under section 135 for that pertinent financial institution; 'subscriber' means any person to whom shares in a pertinent financial institution incorporated in Singapore are, are to be, or are proposed to be, issued under this Division.

sections.title: Compliance with share capital restructuring conditions
sections.summary: Subscribers must comply with all conditions from subsection (12) that they have been given written notice of by the Authority. Contravention of subsection (13) or (14) is an offence punishable by fines up to $250,000 for individuals and $250,000 for entities, with additional daily fines for continuing offences. The Authority may issue certificates of share capital restructuring that specify cancellations of share capital, share issuance details, and other matters. The certificate must be served on the financial institution and published in the Gazette. The certificate takes effect immediately upon issuance and binds all affected parties. Failure to comply with the certificate results in offences with fines up to $250,000 for entities and $125,000 for individuals, with daily fines for continuing offences. The Authority may appoint persons to assess the certificate's implementation and provide reports to the Authority. The Division 6 financial institution must comply with all conditions in the certificate.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for share capital restructuring certificates that must be followed by financial institutions, including specific obligations for compliance, publication, and enforcement. These conditions impact data transfer when restructuring involves customer or financial data.

requirements.text: The subscriber must comply with every condition referred to in subsection (12) that applies to the subscriber and of which the subscriber has been given written notice by the Authority.

A person that contravenes subsection (13) or (14) shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction to a fine not exceeding $250,000 and, in the case of a continuing offence, to a further fine not exceeding $25,000 for every day or part of a day during which the offence continues after conviction.
The Minister must issue a certificate of restructuring of share capital as soon as practicable after approving a determination.
The certificate must specify such information as may be prescribed by regulations made under section 135.
The certificate may make provision for cancellation of whole or part of share capital not paid up.
The certificate may make provision for cancellation of whole or part of paid-up share capital lost or not represented by available assets.
The certificate may specify shares to be issued, consideration, and payment period.
The Minister may add to, vary or revoke any matter specified in the certificate before it comes into effect.
The Authority must serve the certificate and any additions, variations or revocations on the financial institution.
The Authority must publish the certificate in the Gazette and in newspapers as the Minister determines.
The certificate takes effect immediately upon issuance without further action by the financial institution.
The certificate is binding on any person affected by it.
The financial institution must lodge a copy of the certificate with the Registrar of Companies within 7 days after being served.

A financial institution that fails to comply with the certificate is guilty of an offence with fines up to $250,000 for entities and $125,000 for individuals, with daily fines for continuing offences.
A person charged with an offence under subsection (9) may claim ignorance of the contravention as a defence if they were unaware and complied within a reasonable time.
The Authority may appoint persons to assess the certificate's implementation and provide reports to the Authority.

sections.source_quote: The subscriber must comply with every condition referred to in subsection (12) that applies to the subscriber and of which the subscriber has been given written notice by the Authority.

sections.title: Conditions for determining foreign resolution recognition
sections.summary: This section details the conditions under which the Monetary Authority of Singapore (MAS) must determine whether to recognize a foreign resolution. It requires that recognition would not have widespread adverse effects on Singapore's financial system, would not result in inequitable treatment of creditors or shareholders, would not be contrary to national or public interest, would not have material fiscal implications for Singapore, and would comply with any prescribed conditions. These conditions ensure that cross-border financial resolutions are handled in a manner that protects Singapore's economic interests.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets conditions for recognizing foreign financial resolutions, ensuring that cross-border data transfers related to financial resolutions do not adversely affect Singapore's financial system or create inequitable treatment of stakeholders.

requirements.text: The Authority must make a determination that the foreign resolution should be recognised in whole or in part or should not be recognised, based on the conditions that recognition would not have widespread adverse effects on Singapore's financial system or economy, would not result in inequitable treatment of Singapore creditors or shareholders, would not be contrary to national or public interest, would not have material fiscal implications for Singapore, and would comply with any prescribed conditions

sections.source_quote: 103.—(3) The Authority may make a determination that the foreign resolution should be recognised in whole or in part if it is satisfied that all of the following conditions are fulfilled: (a) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not have a widespread adverse effect on the financial system in Singapore or the economy of Singapore, whether or not that effect occurs directly or indirectly as a result of the effects of recognising the resolution or part; (b) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not result in inequitable treatment of any Singapore creditor relative to any other creditor of the foreign financial institution with similar rights, or of any Singapore shareholder relative to any shareholder of the foreign financial institution; (c) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not be contrary to the national interest or public interest; (d) recognition of the foreign resolution or part would not have material fiscal implications for Singapore; (e) any other condition that is prescribed by regulations made under section 135 for the purposes of this paragraph.
sections.relevance_reason: MAS conditions for foreign resolution recognition

sections.summary: This section outlines offences for persons who refuse or fail to comply with orders under section 104 or directions under section 105. requirements.text: A person that refuses or fails to comply with a provision of the order under section 104 that applies to the person, or a direction issued to the person under section 105, shall be guilty of an offence and shall be liable on conviction — (a) in the case of an individual, to a fine not exceeding $125,000 or to imprisonment for a term not exceeding 3 years or to both and, in the case of a continuing offence, to a further fine not exceeding $12,500 for every day or part of a day during which the offence continues after conviction; or (b) in any other case, to a fine not exceeding $250,000 and, in the case of a continuing offence, to a further fine not exceeding $25,000 for every day or part of a day during which the offence continues after conviction

sections.title: Recovery of levies and late payment fees from resolution fund
sections.summary: This section establishes that levies and late payment fees collected from financial institutions or market infrastructure under specific sections are recoverable as debts from the relevant trustee of the resolution fund. It also mandates that any excess levies paid by individuals must be refunded by the trustee, and allows the trustee to seek refunds with ministerial approval under certain conditions. The trustee may also recover costs from levy payers who have initiated legal proceedings to recover these amounts.
sections.dtia_summary: This section specifies conditions under which levies and late payment fees can be recovered by resolution fund trustees, impacting cross-border financial obligations and refund mechanisms for international financial institutions.

requirements.text: The levy imposed on a person under section 113(3), (5) or (6), and any late payment fee imposed on the person under section 114(3), are both recoverable as a debt due from that person to the trustee of the resolution fund concerned.

The amount of levy that a market infrastructure is required to collect from its participants under section 115(2), and any late payment fee imposed on the market infrastructure under section 115(4), are both recoverable as a debt due from the market infrastructure to the trustee of the resolution fund concerned.

Where the trustee of a resolution fund has commenced any legal proceedings in a court in Singapore to recover any levy or late payment fee from a person, the trustee is entitled to claim costs on a full indemnity basis from that person.
All levies and late payment fees collected or recovered are to be paid into the resolution fund concerned.

Where a levy payer has paid an amount of levy that is in excess of the amount imposed on the levy payer under a notice under section 114, the trustee of the resolution fund concerned must make a withdrawal from the fund to refund the excess amount to the levy payer.

In any particular case other than the one to which subsection (5) applies, the trustee of a resolution fund may, with the approval of the Minister, make a withdrawal from the resolution fund to refund in whole or in part any levy paid by a levy payer.

In any particular case other than the one to which subsection (5) applies, the trustee of a resolution fund may, with the approval of the Minister, remit in whole or in part any levy payable by a levy payer.

sections.source_quote: 116.—(1) The levy imposed on a person under section 113(3), (5) or (6), and any late payment fee imposed on the person under section 114(3), are both recoverable as a debt due from that person to the trustee of the resolution fund concerned.

sections.title: Priority of resolution fund debts over unsecured liabilities
sections.summary: This section states that in the event of a financial institution's winding up (excluding certain banks and insurers), debts claimed by the resolution fund trustee under section 112 and levies imposed under sections 113, 114, or 115 have priority over all unsecured liabilities except those specified in section 203(1) of the Insolvency Act. This ensures resolution fund debts are paid before other creditors in insolvency proceedings.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes that resolution fund debts take priority over unsecured liabilities in financial institutions' insolvency, directly impacting data transfer and resolution fund obligations across borders.

requirements.text: Any sum claimed by the trustee of a resolution fund from the financial institution under section 112 has priority over all unsecured liabilities of the financial institution other than preferential debts specified in section 203(1) of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018.

Any levy and late payment imposed on the financial institution under section 113, 114 or 115 and due from the financial institution, and any levy which the financial institution is liable to collect under section 115(2) and due from the financial institution, have priority over all unsecured liabilities of the financial institution other than preferential debts specified in section 203(1) of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018.

sections.source_quote: 119. Despite any written law or rule of law relating to the winding up of companies, in the event of a winding up of a financial institution (other than one that is a bank, a finance company licensed under the Finance Companies Act 1967 or an insurer licensed under the Insurance Act 1966) — any sum claimed by the trustee of a resolution fund from the financial institution under section 112; and any levy and late payment imposed on the financial institution under section 113, 114 or 115 and due from the financial institution, and any levy which the financial institution is liable to collect under section 115(2) and due from the financial institution, have priority over all unsecured liabilities of the financial institution other than preferential debts specified in section 203(1) of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018.

sections.title: Prohibition order continuation after repeal date
sections.summary: This section specifies that a prohibition order under the Securities and Futures Act 2001 continues to apply to a person if the Authority provided them with an opportunity to be heard before the repeal date and did not make the order or inform them of non-making the order. The order remains effective as if certain sections of the Financial Services and Markets Act 2022 had not been enacted.

sections.title: Definitions for legal and financial terms
sections.summary: This section clarifies the meanings of specific terms used in the previous section, including 'advocate and solicitor', 'practising certificate', 'regulated foreign lawyer', 'Official Assignee', 'Official Receiver', 'sub-fund', and 'VCC' as defined in relevant Acts such as the Legal Profession Act 1966 and Variable Capital Companies Act 2018.

requirements.text: "advocate and solicitor", "practising certificate" and "regulated foreign lawyer" have the meanings given by section 2(1) of the Legal Profession Act 1966
"Official Assignee" and "Official Receiver" have the meanings given by section 2(1) of the Insolvency, Restructuring and Dissolution Act 2018
"sub-fund" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Variable Capital Companies Act 2018
"VCC" has the meaning given by section 2(1) of the Variable Capital Companies Act 2018

sections.source_quote: 2.
